title: The I3D Architecture (Inflated 3D)
concept: I3D (Inflated 3D) converts successful 2D architectures into 3D models by inflating kernels, bootstrapping weights from ImageNet.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the primary input to the temporal stream of a Two-Stream network?
exercises.explain: Yes! The temporal stream processes pre-calculated optical flow fields.

cards.title: Inflating a balloon
cards.body: It takes millions of images to train a deep 2D model like Inception. But video datasets are much smaller. How can we get the power of deep 2D models without starting from scratch? We can inflate a flat 2D network like a balloon, turning its 2D filters into 3D filters!

cards.title: I3D (Inflated 3D)
cards.body: I3D (Inflated 3D ConvNet) is an architecture that takes a successful 2D network (like Inception-v1) and inflates all its 2D filters ($k imes k$) into 3D filters ($k imes k imes k$). It bootstraps training by repeating the pre-trained 2D ImageNet weights along the time axis.

cards.title: Extruding play-doh
cards.body: Imagine squeezing Play-Doh through a square mold. You get a long square log. Inflation is like taking a flat 2D filter (the square mold) and extruding it along the time axis to create a solid 3D filter (the log), inheriting the shape features.

cards.title: Weight inflation math
cards.body: If a 2D filter has a weight $w$, we inflate it to a 3D temporal size of 3 by repeating the weight: $[w/3, w/3, w/3]$. Dividing by 3 ensures the output activations remain identical to the original 2D network on static images!

cards.title: Not starting from zero
cards.body: You might think 3D networks must always be trained from scratch. I3D proves we can reuse **transfer learning** from 2D image models! Bootstrapping 2D weights from ImageNet gives the 3D model an immediate head start, accelerating training.

cards.title: 2D Filter vs. Inflated 3D Filter
cards.body: Comparing filter shapes and weight distribution:

left.text:
Weights: $W$
Trained on ImageNet (2D)
Processes a single image frame

right.text:
Weights: $[W/3, W/3, W/3]$
Bootstrapped from 2D weights
Processes a volume of frames

exercises.prompt: What does the 'I' in I3D stand for?
exercises.explain: Correct! I3D stands for Inflated 3D ConvNet, referring to the inflation of 2D layers into 3D layers.

exercises.prompt: True or false: When inflating a 2D filter of weight W into a 3x3x3 filter, we divide the weights by the temporal size (3) to preserve activation scale.
exercises.explain: True. Dividing the weights by the temporal kernel size ensures that a static video input produces the exact same activation values as a single 2D image.

exercises.sentence: I3D allows us to leverage transfer learning by bootstrapping weights from pre-trained ___ models.
exercises.explain: I3D reuse weights from models pre-trained on large 2D image datasets like ImageNet.

exercises.prompt: Which architecture was originally inflated to create the standard I3D network?
exercises.explain: The original I3D model was created by inflating the Inception-v1 architecture.

exercises.prompt: Why is bootstrapping pre-trained weights so helpful for video models like I3D?

options.text: Because video datasets are relatively small, and pre-trained image weights provide strong baseline object detection features.
Because it automatically reduces the size of video files.
Because pre-trained weights make 3D networks run without using any electricity.
Because it converts 3D convolutions back into 2D convolutions.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Video datasets with clean labels are hard to collect. Reusing weights from 2D ImageNet training gives the model a massive head start in understanding shapes and textures. Next: how do we compare all these video models? Next up: Comparing Video Models.
